announcements.title: Welcome to The Ali Collegates LMS (TACLMS)
announcements.body: Welcome students and teachers! This portal will house your class notes, recorded video lectures, assignments, and mini-quizzes. Ensure your GR number is accurate and report any subject access discrepancies directly to Prof. Ali.

announcements.title: Weekly Attendance and Quiz Reminders
announcements.body: Starting this week, quizzes will be posted for each chapter. Completing notes and scoring high on quizzes will grant you XP to level up. Keep tracking your progress bar!

quizzes.title: Kinematics Fundamental MCQ Quiz
quizzes.description: Quick check of distance, velocity, acceleration, and equations of motion.

questions.question: What is the rate of change of displacement called?
questions.explanation: Velocity is defined as displacement per unit time.

questions.question: If a body starts from rest, its initial velocity (u) is:
questions.explanation: Rest implies velocity is zero at the start.

questions.question: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
questions.explanation: Speed only has magnitude, while displacement, acceleration, and force have both magnitude and direction.

notes.title: Chapter 1 Study Notes: Physical Quantities & Measurement
notes.content:
===================================================
THE ALI'S COLLEGIATE — CORE COMPREHENSIVE NOTES
SUBJECT: PHYSICS (CLASS 9TH)
CHAPTER 1: PHYSICAL QUANTITIES & MEASUREMENT
===================================================

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION A: CONCEPTUAL QUESTIONS & ANSWERS
---------------------------------------------------

Q1. What is the difference between Base and Derived Quantities?
Ans:
- Base Quantities: The quantities on the basis of which other quantities are expressed. There are 7 base quantities in total:
  1. Length (Meter - m)
  2. Mass (Kilogram - kg)
  3. Time (Second - s)
  4. Electric Current (Ampere - A)
  5. Intensity of Light (Candela - cd)
  6. Temperature (Kelvin - K)
  7. Amount of Substance (Mole - mol)

- Derived Quantities: The quantities that are expressed in terms of base quantities. Examples:
  1. Force (Newton - N = kg·m/s²)
  2. Area (Square Meter - m²)
  3. Volume (Cubic Meter - m³)
  4. Speed / Velocity (Meter per second - m/s)
  5. Pressure (Pascal - Pa = N/m²)

Q2. What is a Vernier Calliper? How is its Least Count derived?
Ans: A Vernier Calliper is an instrument used to measure small lengths, internal/external diameters, or depth of objects up to 0.1 mm accuracy.
- Derivation of Least Count (LC):
  LC is the difference between one main scale division (1 mm) and one vernier scale division (0.9 mm).
  Formula: LC = (Value of smallest division on main scale) / (Total number of divisions on vernier scale)
  Smallest main scale division = 1 mm
  Total divisions on Vernier scale = 10 divisions
  LC = 1 mm / 10 = 0.1 mm = 0.01 cm.

Q3. Explain Zero Error and Zero Correction in Vernier Callipers.
Ans:
- Zero Error: When the jaws of the vernier callipers are closed, if the zero line of the vernier scale does not coincide with the zero line of the main scale, a zero error exists.
- Positive Zero Error: If the vernier zero is to the right side of the main scale zero. (Subtract this error from the final reading).
- Negative Zero Error: If the vernier zero is to the left side of the main scale zero. (Add this error to the final reading).
- Zero Correction (Z.C.): It is the negative of the Zero Error. Z.C. = - (Zero Error).

Q4. What is a Screw Gauge (Micrometer)? How is its Least Count calculated?
Ans: A Screw Gauge is a high-precision instrument used to measure extremely small thicknesses or wire diameters up to 0.01 mm accuracy.
- Calculation of Least Count (LC):
  Formula: LC = (Pitch of the Screw Gauge) / (Number of divisions on circular scale)
  If Pitch = 0.5 mm and Circular scale divisions = 50:
  LC = 0.5 mm / 50 = 0.01 mm = 0.001 cm.

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION B: SIGNIFICANT FIGURES RULES
---------------------------------------------------
1. All non-zero digits are significant (e.g., 438 has 3 significant figures).
2. Zeros between non-zero digits are significant (e.g., 2005 has 4 significant figures).
3. Leading zeros (to the left of the first non-zero digit) are NOT significant (e.g., 0.0034 has 2 significant figures).
4. Trailing zeros in a decimal number are significant (e.g., 4.500 has 4 significant figures).

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION C: SOLVED BOARD NUMERICAL PROBLEMS
---------------------------------------------------

Problem 1.1: A student measures the diameter of a wire using a screw gauge of pitch 0.5 mm and 100 circular scale divisions. When jaws are closed, circular zero is at the 4th division past index line. What is the Least Count and the Zero Error?
Solution:
Given:
  Pitch = 0.5 mm
  Circular scale divisions (N) = 100
  1. Least Count (LC) = Pitch / N = 0.5 mm / 100 = 0.005 mm = 0.0005 cm.
  2. Since circular zero is at 4th division (past zero index):
     Zero Error = + (4 * LC) = + (4 * 0.005 mm) = +0.02 mm (Positive zero error).
     Zero Correction (Z.C.) = -0.02 mm.

Problem 1.2: Identify the number of significant figures in:
  a) 0.00234 kg -> Answer: 3 (digits 2, 3, 4)
  b) 4.005 m -> Answer: 4 (including trapped zeros)
  c) 1.40 * 10^-5 s -> Answer: 3 (scientific notation digits 1, 4, 0)

notes.title: Chapter 2 Study Notes: Kinematics & Equations of Motion
notes.content:
===================================================
THE ALI'S COLLEGIATE — CORE COMPREHENSIVE NOTES
SUBJECT: PHYSICS (CLASS 9TH)
CHAPTER 2: KINEMATICS
===================================================

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION A: CORE DEFINITIONS & CONCEPTS
---------------------------------------------------

1. Kinematics: The study of motion of an object without discussing the cause of motion.
2. Rest vs Motion:
   - Rest: A body is at rest if it does not change its position with respect to its surroundings.
   - Motion: A body is in motion if it changes its position with respect to its surroundings.
   - Rest and motion are relative states. For example, a passenger inside a moving bus is at rest relative to other passengers, but in motion relative to a bystander outside.

3. Types of Motion:
   - Translatory: Body moves along a line without rotation (e.g., car moving on a straight road, circular motion).
   - Rotatory: Spinning motion of a body about its axis (e.g., rotation of the Earth, spinning top).
   - Vibratory: To-and-fro (oscillatory) motion about a mean position (e.g., simple pendulum, cradle swing).

4. Scalars and Vectors:
   - Scalar: Quantities described completely by magnitude only (e.g., Mass, Distance, Speed, Work, Temperature).
   - Vector: Quantities described completely by both magnitude and direction (e.g., Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration, Force, Momentum).

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION B: MATH DERIVATIONS (THREE EQUATIONS OF MOTION)
---------------------------------------------------
These equations apply to bodies moving with uniform acceleration 'a' along a straight line.
Let 'vi' be the initial velocity, 'vf' be the final velocity, 't' be time, and 'S' be displacement.

Method: Speed-Time Graphical Analysis
Consider a body moving with initial velocity vi at point A, accelerating uniformly to final velocity vf at point B in time t.
Slope of line AB represents acceleration 'a'.
Area under line AB represents distance 'S'.

Derivation 1: First Equation of Motion (vf = vi + at)
- Acceleration 'a' is the slope of line AB.
  Slope 'a' = (BC) / (AC)
  From graph, BC = BD - CD = vf - vi
  And AC = OD = t
  Therefore:
  a = (vf - vi) / t
  at = vf - vi
  vf = vi + at   [PROVED]

Derivation 2: Second Equation of Motion (S = vi*t + 1/2 * a * t²)
- Total distance S is equal to the total area of OABD under the slope line AB.
  Area of OABD = Area of rectangle OACD + Area of triangle ABC
  1. Area of rectangle OACD = Length * Width = OD * OA = t * vi
  2. Area of triangle ABC = 1/2 * Base * Height = 1/2 * AC * BC = 1/2 * t * (at)  (since BC = at)
  Summing the areas:
  S = vi*t + 1/2 * t * (at)
  S = vi*t + 1/2 * a * t²   [PROVED]

Derivation 3: Third Equation of Motion (2aS = vf² - vi²)
- Total distance S is equal to the area of trapezoid OABD.
  Area of trapezoid S = 1/2 * (Sum of parallel sides) * (Perpendicular height)
  S = 1/2 * (OA + BD) * OD
  S = 1/2 * (vi + vf) * t
  Multiply both sides by acceleration slope conversion ratio (BC / AC) which is equivalent to 'a' on LHS:
  a * S = ( (vf + vi) / 2 ) * ( (vf - vi) / t ) * t
  aS = (vf² - vi²) / 2
  2aS = vf² - vi²   [PROVED]

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION C: SOLVED BOARD NUMERICAL PROBLEMS
---------------------------------------------------

Problem 2.1: A train starts from rest. It moves through 1 km in 100 seconds with uniform acceleration. What will be its final speed?
Solution:
Given:
  Initial velocity (vi) = 0 m/s (starts from rest)
  Distance (S) = 1 km = 1000 m
  Time (t) = 100 s
To find:
  Final velocity (vf) = ?
Step 1: Find acceleration 'a' using Second Equation:
  S = vi*t + 1/2 * a * t²
  1000 = (0 * 100) + 1/2 * a * (100)²
  1000 = 1/2 * a * 10000
  1000 = 5000 * a
  a = 1000 / 5000 = 0.2 m/s²
Step 2: Find final speed using First Equation:
  vf = vi + at
  vf = 0 + (0.2 * 100)
  vf = 20 m/s.
Answer: The final speed of the train is 20 m/s.

notes.title: Chapter 3 Study Notes: Dynamics & Forces
notes.content:
===================================================
THE ALI'S COLLEGIATE — CORE COMPREHENSIVE NOTES
SUBJECT: PHYSICS (CLASS 9TH)
CHAPTER 3: DYNAMICS
===================================================

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION A: CORE DEFINITIONS & CONCEPTS
---------------------------------------------------

1. Dynamics: The branch of mechanics that deals with the study of motion of objects and the causes of their motion (Forces).
2. Force: An agent that moves or tends to move, stops or tends to stop the motion of a body, or changes its direction/shape. (SI Unit: Newton - N).
3. Inertia: The property of a body due to which it resists any change in its state of rest or motion. Galileo concluded that mass is the quantitative measure of inertia.
4. Momentum: The quantity of motion possessed by a body due to its mass and velocity.
   Formula: p = m * v   (SI Unit: kg·m/s or N·s).

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION B: NEWTON'S LAWS OF MOTION
---------------------------------------------------

- Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia):
  A body continues in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by a net force.
  Example: When a moving bus stops suddenly, the passengers fall forward because the lower parts of their bodies stop with the bus, while their upper parts continue in motion due to inertia.

- Newton's Second Law (F = ma):
  When a net force acts on a body, it produces acceleration in the body in the direction of the net force. This acceleration is directly proportional to the force and inversely proportional to the mass of the body.
  Derivation:
  Acceleration 'a' is proportional to Force 'F' (a ∝ F)
  Acceleration 'a' is inversely proportional to Mass 'm' (a ∝ 1/m)
  Combining: a ∝ F / m
  F ∝ m * a
  F = k * m * a   (where k is constant, k = 1 in SI units)
  F = m * a       [PROVED]

- Newton's Third Law (Action and Reaction):
  To every action there is always an equal and opposite reaction.
  Example: Rocket propulsion - burning gases escape downwards (action), pushing the rocket upwards (reaction).

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION C: VERTICAL MOTION OF BODIES ON A PULLEY (ATWOOD MACHINE)
---------------------------------------------------
Consider two bodies of masses m1 and m2 (where m1 > m2) connected to the ends of an inextensible string passing over a frictionless pulley.

For Mass m1 (Moving Downwards):
- Net downward force = Weight - Tension
  m1 * g - T = m1 * a   --- (Equation 1)

For Mass m2 (Moving Upwards):
- Net upward force = Tension - Weight
  T - m2 * g = m2 * a   --- (Equation 2)

Derivation for Acceleration 'a':
- Add Equation 1 and Equation 2:
  (m1 * g - T) + (T - m2 * g) = m1 * a + m2 * a
  m1 * g - m2 * g = (m1 + m2) * a
  g * (m1 - m2) = (m1 + m2) * a
  a = ( (m1 - m2) / (m1 + m2) ) * g   [PROVED]

Derivation for Tension 'T':
- Divide Equation 1 by Equation 2:
  (m1 * g - T) / (T - m2 * g) = m1 / m2
  m2 * (m1 * g - T) = m1 * (T - m2 * g)
  m1 * m2 * g - m2 * T = m1 * T - m1 * m2 * g
  2 * m1 * m2 * g = T * (m1 + m2)
  T = ( (2 * m1 * m2) / (m1 + m2) ) * g   [PROVED]

---------------------------------------------------
SECTION D: SOLVED BOARD NUMERICAL PROBLEMS
---------------------------------------------------

Problem 3.1: Two masses 52 kg and 48 kg are attached to the ends of a string that passes over a frictionless pulley. Find the acceleration in the system and the tension in the string when both masses are moving vertically. (g = 10 m/s²)
Solution:
Given:
  m1 = 52 kg
  m2 = 48 kg
  g = 10 m/s²
1. Acceleration 'a':
  a = [ (m1 - m2) / (m1 + m2) ] * g
  a = [ (52 - 48) / (52 + 48) ] * 10
  a = [ 4 / 100 ] * 10 = 0.4 m/s²
2. Tension 'T':
  T = [ (2 * m1 * m2) / (m1 + m2) ] * g
  T = [ (2 * 52 * 48) / (52 + 48) ] * 10
  T = [ 4992 / 100 ] * 10 = 499.2 N.
Answer: The acceleration is 0.4 m/s² and the tension is 499.2 N.
